range.conservationStatus: Secure — economically and ecologically important; sensitive to climate and deer browse
range.geographicRange: Eastern North America — dominant canopy tree in northern hardwood forests

elevationRange.note: Common on cool slopes and ravines in Pike County; sugar bush operations occur on accessible north slopes

ecology.role: Foundation canopy species — structures northern hardwood forests, supports diverse understory when deer pressure is moderate, and provides sap, timber, and autumn foliage that drives regional phenology education.

associatedSpecies.relationship: Mycorrhizal mushrooms on maple forest floors
Nesting and foraging in maple edges

habitat.summary: Cool, moist, well-drained soils on north and east slopes — mature northern hardwood forest and sugar bush stands.
preferred: Mixed stands with beech and basswood
habitat.substrate: Mineral soil with rich humus layer; seedlings need forest gap or edge light

phenology.seasonality: Deciduous — annual leaf, flower, and seed cycle drives forest calendar
phenology.activePeriod: Sap flow: late February–March (freeze-thaw). Leaves: May. Fall color: October. Samaras drop: autumn.

regionalNotes.pike-county-pa: Compare bud break on south vs. north slope maples — a standard phenology transect for schools.

identification.summary: Large hardwood with opposite, palmately lobed leaves — five lobes with U-shaped sinuses between lobes (vs. sharp V in red maple).
fieldMarks: Opposite branching — every twig has a partner across the stem
Leaves: 5 lobes, smooth margin, U-shaped notches between lobes
Bark on mature trees: dark gray, vertically furrowed plates
Winged seeds (samaras) in pairs, angle less than 90°
Bud scales brownish, pointed — compare to red maple's red buds

lookAlikes.name: Red maple (Acer rubrum)
lookAlikes.distinction: V-shaped sinuses, serrated leaf margins, red buds and flower stems

lookAlikes.name: Norway maple (introduced)
lookAlikes.distinction: Milky sap from broken leaf petiole; wider leaf, invasive in some areas

considerations: Sap collection requires landowner permission and proper boiling equipment
Dead branches (widow makers) — look up before standing under canopy in wind

observation: Tag or photograph the same tree weekly for phenology — no need to collect leaves
Do not tap trees on public land without authorization
Collect one leaf for press book at home — not entire branches from park trees
leaveNoTrace: Stay on trails in sugar bush operations during sap season
Do not carve bark or hammer nails into living trees
ethics.conservation: Sugar maple regeneration is threatened by deer browse and climate stress in some regions — observation supports stewardship, not extraction.

tips: Bark close-ups teach aging — young smooth vs. mature plate bark
Wide shots show canopy role in forest structure

education.overview: Sugar maple is the tree that teaches opposite branching, freeze-thaw sap flow, and why northern hardwood forests feel different from oak ridges.
education.summary: Learn U-shaped leaf sinuses and opposite twigs — then you can name the forest type.
interestingFacts: Sap is only 2–3% sugar — forty gallons boil to one gallon of syrup
Fall color varies by tree genetics and summer drought — not uniform across a stand
Maple is among the most valuable timber species in the Northeast
fieldNotes: Count lobes and measure sinus shape on five leaves
Date bud break, full leaf, and first fall color on a marked tree
Note deer browse on seedlings below parent canopy

relatedSpecies.relationship: Different kingdom — but shares terrace and slope ecology stories

futureObservations: Weekly phenology photos of the same individual tree
Seedling survival under varying browse pressure
Sap run start date vs. freeze-thaw cycle
unknowns: Climate-driven range shifts are projected — local long-term data needed
Individual trees vary in sap sugar content — anecdotal sweetness is not universal

media.alt: Sugar maple canopy in autumn northern hardwood forest
media.caption: Opposite branching and five-lobed leaves — placeholder

prompts: Phenology stage: bud, leaf, flower, fruit, fall color
Slope aspect and associated canopy species

research.notes: Anchor species for phenology education and forest moisture lessons in ForageCast
